nav.linkAccountFirst: Link a Lichess or Chess.com username in Settings first

landing.heroTitle: Turn your games into a coach.
landing.heroBody: ChessInt is chess intelligence for your own games. Connect Lichess or Chess.com, and get full engine analysis, the weaknesses you repeat, and a personal AI coach — free, right in your browser.
landing.feature1Body: Stockfish runs in your browser and evaluates every move of every game — no server queue, no waiting.
landing.feature2Body: Not one-off blunders — the mistakes you repeat, broken down by phase, tactical motif, and opening.
landing.feature3Body: See which of your lines actually win and which quietly lose, with real win-rates from your own games.
landing.feature4Body: A written report that reads like a coach who studied hundreds of your games, with drills to fix each gap.
landing.stepsTitle: From 500 games to one clear plan
landing.step1Body: Link your Lichess or Chess.com username.
landing.step2Body: ChessInt reviews your full game history with the engine.
landing.step3Body: Get your weaknesses, an AI coaching report, and targeted puzzles.
landing.faq1A: ChessInt (short for Chess Intelligence) is a free web app that analyzes your own Chess.com and Lichess games. It runs engine analysis on your whole history, gives every move a game review, finds the weaknesses you repeat, and writes you a personal AI coaching report.
landing.faq2A: Yes. Engine analysis, the move-by-move game review, weakness detection, and puzzles are free with no game limit. The engine runs in your browser, so there is no analysis queue.
landing.faq3Q: Does ChessInt work with both Chess.com and Lichess?
landing.faq3A: Yes. Connect a Chess.com username, a Lichess username, or both, and ChessInt imports and analyzes your games from either platform.
landing.faq4Q: How is ChessInt different from the Chess.com game review?
landing.faq4A: ChessInt's game review is free and unlimited, needs no membership, works with Lichess too, and looks across your entire history to find the mistakes you repeat — not just one game at a time.
landing.faq5Q: Is ChessInt the same as Chessnut?
landing.faq5A: No. ChessInt is a free web app for analyzing your online chess games on Chess.com and Lichess. Chessnut is an unrelated brand of physical electronic chessboards.
landing.closingTitle: Find out what's really costing you rating.

settings.title: Settings
settings.emailHint: Your account email cannot be changed.

accuracy.short: How close your moves were to the best moves, scored 0–100%. Higher is better — 90%+ is excellent, under 70% means several moves went wrong.
accuracy.long: Accuracy rolls every move of a game into one friendly number. It is built from how much each move changed your chance of winning, not from raw engine points, so it is easy to compare between games.

cpl.short: How much a move 'cost' you, measured in 1/100ths of a pawn. 0 is perfect; under ~20 is fine; big numbers mean a big mistake.
cpl.long: Centipawn Loss is the gap between the move you played and the best available move. Because a pawn is worth 100 centipawns, a CPL of 50 means you gave up about half a pawn of advantage.

acpl.short: Your Centipawn Loss averaged over a whole game (or many games) — a single number for how cleanly you played. Lower is better.
acpl.long: ACPL is the most common way to compare how well two players, or two of your own games, were played. Strong club players often average under 30.

centipawn.short: A unit for measuring advantage: 100 centipawns = the value of one pawn. The engine scores every position this way.

evaluation.short: The engine's score for a position. Positive = you're better, negative = your opponent is. +1.0 ≈ a pawn ahead, +3.0 ≈ a piece ahead.
evaluation.long: The evaluation is the engine's overall judgement of who stands better and by how much, considering material, king safety, activity, and more.

winpct.short: Your estimated chance of winning from a position, based on the engine's evaluation. Easier to read than centipawns.

blunder.short: A serious mistake that badly hurts your position — usually hanging a piece or missing a major threat. ChessInt marks these so you can learn from them.

mistake.short: A clear error that worsens your position — less severe than a blunder, but it still hands your opponent an edge.

inaccuracy.short: A small slip: not losing, but not the best move. A series of these quietly adds up.

brilliant.short: A great, often surprising move — usually a smart sacrifice the engine confirms is best. The hardest classification to earn.

best.short: You played the engine's top choice for that position.

book.short: A known opening move from established theory — solid, but it's memorised theory rather than your own calculation.

miss.short: You had a strong tactic or winning chance available and didn't play it.

opening.short: The first stage of the game (roughly the first 10–15 moves), where you develop your pieces and fight for the centre.

middlegame.short: The main battle after the pieces are developed — plans, attacks, and tactics. Where most games are won or lost.

endgame.short: The final stage, with few pieces left. Technique matters most here: king activity, passed pawns, and known winning methods.

tilt.short: Playing worse because of emotion — frustration after a loss leading to rushed, weaker moves. ChessInt can spot it in your results.

stockfish.short: The free, world-class chess engine ChessInt runs in your browser to analyse your games.

learn.hubTitle: Your chess coach, from the very first move
learn.hubIntro: Whether you have never touched a chessboard or you already play and want to climb, start here. These guides are written to be read and used — no jargon without an explanation, and every term links to a plain-English definition.
learn.improveIntro: ChessInt tells you where you lose rating — your weakest phase, your repeated mistakes. These guides tell you what to do about it.
learn.hubCtaTitle: Ready to see your own games analysed?
learn.howToPlayBody: Never played before? Set up the board the right way, learn how each piece moves, and understand check, checkmate, and the special moves — with diagrams.
learn.notationBody: What 'Nf3' and 'O-O' mean, so the moves in your game review actually make sense.
learn.glossaryCardBody: Plain-English definitions of every term ChessInt uses — accuracy, CPL, blunder, eval, and more.
learn.middlegameBody: The stage where most games are decided. A practical plan for better plans, tactics, and decisions in the thick of the game.
learn.endgameBody: The most trainable part of chess. Learn the handful of endgames that win the most points — and how to convert a winning position.
learn.glossaryIntro: Every term ChessInt uses, explained in plain English. If a word in your report or game review ever stops you, it is defined here.
